results.title: Most users won’t be able to claim their share of Uniswap fees
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit’s Issue 5 describes the exact same flaw in ILOPool.claim(): it calls pool.collect(type(uint128).max, type(uint128).max), sweeping all fees for the shared Uniswap V3 position, then sends the remainder (which includes other users’ fees) to the fee taker. This causes subsequent claim() calls to revert due to insufficient collected tokens, effectively stealing fees and DoSing later claimants. This matches the vulnerability description’s root cause, code path (ILOPool.claim and pool.collect), impact (fee theft and DoS), and suggested fix (collect only the claiming user’s share rather than sweeping all). Fixing the issue as outlined in the report would also fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: Adversary can prevent the launch of any ILO pool with enough raised capital at any moment by providing single-sided liquidity
results.reasoning: Detected. Audit issue #7 describes the same root cause and code path: ILOManager.launch requires the pool’s current sqrtPriceX96 to exactly equal initialPoolPriceX96, enabling a public price manipulation on the Uniswap V3 pool to DoS the launch. The vulnerability description adds a variant (cementing the manipulation with single-sided liquidity), but both exploit the same equality check in ILOManager.launch and lead to the same launch-blocking outcome. A fix that removes/relaxes the strict equality or otherwise controls/sets price at launch would resolve both.
